BodyText: The wreck buckles inward as heat climbs through the ravine. A damaged guidance voice cuts through the static: survive first, explain later. The machine core under your skin responds before thought can catch up.

Iset stares at the scorched trench, still shaking from the blast. She wants the world reduced to something survivable: smoke, panic, heat. But she also knows she watched a body walk out of impossible fire.

In the archive vault, Nesamun opens a chamber where a voice should not exist. The priesthood kept the machine fed with ritual, secrecy, and fear. He asks whether truth should stay sealed, be shared among the worthy, or break the temple open.

Tiaa kneels in dust that should have been river silt. Her kin are being denied grain unless they repeat miracles they do not control. She wants judgment, but not necessarily blood; she wants someone to decide whether order still deserves obedience.

Melantho stands beneath a bronze beacon lashed by wind. She claims the storm ruler still answers, but the signs now arrive as fragments and failed surges. She asks whether power must remain terrifying, become negotiated law, or be revealed as stolen machinery.

Leonteus has climbed to the shrine with armed retainers and a diplomat's smile. He can be sent away with ceremony, bought into a covenant, or humiliated badly enough to make the mountain path run with blood.

Aulus walks the forge-law chamber with measuring cord in one hand and a bronze tally in the other. He believes divinity is only useful if it can be counted, judged, and forced into roads, weights, and punishments. He wants you to choose what kind of order survives.

Vela waits beside stones moved in the night. The dispute is old enough to be sacred now: one field, one road, one accusation too many. She asks for a verdict that keeps the peace, wins by intimidation, or tears the boundary open so everyone sees what law is made of.

The buried bridge lights in layers as every preserved god-voice speaks at once. Their demand is simple and impossible: restore the old mission, live inside the myth you created, dismantle the machine hierarchy, replace the companion intelligence, or accept a broken inheritance that no one fully owns.
You choose return over myth. The machine world that made you still exists somewhere beyond the broken centuries, and you leave behind a handful of cults trying to explain why their gods walked away.
You remain, and history hardens around your presence. Temples, hills, and storms begin to remember you not as a person but as a pattern too large to separate from worship.

You dismantle the hidden engines of divinity. The result is not liberation in a single moment, but a long cascade of fear, argument, grief, and the first honest institutions these regions have seen in generations.
You replace the companion core and take command of the buried system yourself. The gods do not vanish; they become your infrastructure, your burden, and your temptation.

You leave the legacy fractured. No city, shrine, or law code receives the full machine truth. Instead the future inherits shards, each powerful enough to change a culture and incomplete enough to keep it human.
